---
title: "Ch 10 — Heron's Formula"
subtitle: "Area of a triangle from the three sides alone"
lesson-id: "cbse9.herons-formula"
tier: "cbse9"
ncert-class: 9
ncert-chapter: 10
ncert-chapter-title: "Heron's Formula"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["t1.area-perimeter-volume", "cbse9.triangles", "cbse8.squares-and-square-roots"]
unlocks: ["cbse9.surface-areas-and-volumes", "cbse10.areas-related-to-circles"]
applied-domains: ["research", "game-dev"]
estimated-minutes: 35
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you compute the area of a rectangle and a right triangle?
   *(If no, revisit
   [t1.area-perimeter-volume](../../tier-1-foundations/13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd).)*
2. Can you simplify $\sqrt{144}$ and $\sqrt{72} = 6\sqrt{2}$? *(If no,
   revisit
   [cbse8.squares-and-square-roots](../../tier-cbse-08-class-8/05-squares-and-square-roots/index.qmd).)*
3. Can you check the triangle inequality (each pair of sides sums to
   more than the third)? *(If no, revisit
   [cbse9.triangles](../07-triangles/index.qmd).)*

Three yes? Read on.
:::

## Touch

Take a piece of grid paper. Cut out a triangle with sides exactly $3$,
$4$, $5$ (count the squares carefully). The angle between the two
shorter sides is a right angle. Use the basic formula:
$$\text{Area} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \text{base} \cdot \text{height} =
\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 3 \cdot 4 = 6.$$

Now cut a triangle with sides $5, 5, 6$. There is no right angle — but
you still expect it to have an area. How can you find it without
measuring the height?

```{ojs}
viewof aSide = Inputs.range([3, 12], {value: 5, step: 1, label: "side a"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof bSide = Inputs.range([3, 12], {value: 5, step: 1, label: "side b"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof cSide = Inputs.range([3, 12], {value: 6, step: 1, label: "side c"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const a = aSide, b = bSide, c = cSide;
  const valid = (a + b > c) && (a + c > b) && (b + c > a);
  if (!valid) return html`<div style="color:#a30; font-family: monospace;">${a}, ${b}, ${c}: triangle inequality fails.</div>`;
  const s = (a + b + c) / 2;
  const A = Math.sqrt(s*(s-a)*(s-b)*(s-c));
  return html`<div style="font-family: monospace; font-size: 1rem; padding: 0.4rem 0;">
    sides: ${a}, ${b}, ${c} — semi-perimeter s = ${s}<br/>
    area = √(s(s−a)(s−b)(s−c)) = √(${s}·${s-a}·${s-b}·${s-c}) ≈ ${A.toFixed(4)}
  </div>`;
}
```

Drag the sliders. Whenever the three sides actually make a triangle, you
get a single positive area — without ever drawing or measuring an
altitude.

## Wonder

1. The area of a $3, 4, 5$ triangle is $6$. Why does the formula in the
   widget agree? Plug in: $s = 6$, then $\sqrt{6 \cdot 3 \cdot 2 \cdot 1}$.
2. If you fix the three sides, can the triangle have *more than one*
   area? Could you reshape it?
3. The formula has a peculiar shape: a square root, four factors. Why
   *four* — what would the formula look like for a quadrilateral?
4. The formula was attributed to Heron of Alexandria around 60 CE. Why
   is it strange that this idea waited until then to be discovered?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## A whisper, no answer yet

Q1 is your first sanity check. Q2 is the deep one — three sides
**uniquely** determine a triangle (SSS), so its area is also forced.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 (Sanity check)

Verify Heron's formula for the right triangle with sides $3, 4, 5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Compute $s = (3+4+5)/2 = 6$. Then $s - a, s - b, s - c$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$s = 6$. $s - 3 = 3, s - 4 = 2, s - 5 = 1$. Area $=
\sqrt{6 \cdot 3 \cdot 2 \cdot 1} = \sqrt{36} = 6$. Matches the
$\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 3 \cdot 4 = 6$ from the basic formula. ✓
</details>

### Problem 2 (Equilateral)

Verify Heron's formula for the equilateral triangle with side $a$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$s = 3a/2$. Each $s - $ side $= a/2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$s = \tfrac{3a}{2}$, $s - a = \tfrac{a}{2}$. Area $=
\sqrt{\tfrac{3a}{2} \cdot \tfrac{a}{2} \cdot \tfrac{a}{2} \cdot \tfrac{a}{2}}
= \sqrt{\tfrac{3 a^4}{16}} = \tfrac{a^2 \sqrt{3}}{4}$. This is the
familiar formula for an equilateral triangle's area.
</details>

### Problem 3 (Isosceles)

Find the area of a triangle with sides $5, 5, 6$ using Heron's formula.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$s = 8$. $s - 5 = 3, s - 5 = 3, s - 6 = 2$. Area $=
\sqrt{8 \cdot 3 \cdot 3 \cdot 2} = \sqrt{144} = 12$ square units.
</details>

### Problem 4 (Heron's idea)

Compute Heron's formula for a triangle with sides $7, 8, 9$. Then
compute the height onto the side of length $9$ using
$\text{Area} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \text{base} \cdot \text{height}$. Are
the two consistent?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$s = 12$. Compute Heron's area first; then equate to $\tfrac{1}{2}
\cdot 9 \cdot h$ to find $h$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$s = 12$. Area $= \sqrt{12 \cdot 5 \cdot 4 \cdot 3} = \sqrt{720} = 12
\sqrt{5}$. Setting $\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 9 \cdot h = 12 \sqrt{5}$ gives
$h = \tfrac{24 \sqrt{5}}{9} = \tfrac{8 \sqrt{5}}{3}$. Numerically
$\approx 5.96$. Both formulae deliver the same area; Heron's just skips
the height step.
</details>

### Problem 5 (Quadrilateral by triangulation)

A quadrilateral $ABCD$ has sides $AB = 4$, $BC = 5$, $CD = 6$, $DA = 7$,
and one diagonal $AC = 5$. Find the area by splitting into two triangles
and using Heron's formula on each.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\triangle ABC$ has sides $4, 5, 5$. $\triangle ACD$ has sides $5, 6, 7$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\triangle ABC$: $s = 7$, area $= \sqrt{7 \cdot 3 \cdot 2 \cdot 2} =
\sqrt{84} = 2\sqrt{21} \approx 9.165$.
$\triangle ACD$: $s = 9$, area $= \sqrt{9 \cdot 4 \cdot 3 \cdot 2} =
\sqrt{216} = 6\sqrt{6} \approx 14.697$.
Total area $\approx 23.86$ square units.
</details>

You discovered: Heron's formula always works once the three sides obey
the triangle inequality; it agrees with $\tfrac{1}{2} bh$ but doesn't
need the height; quadrilaterals reduce to two triangles when one
diagonal is known.

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Heron's formula.** A triangle with sides $a, b, c$ has area
$$\text{Area} = \sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)},$$
where $s = \tfrac{a + b + c}{2}$ is the **semi-perimeter**.

The formula uses *only the three sides*. No height, no angle.

For an equilateral triangle of side $a$, this reduces to
$\dfrac{a^2 \sqrt{3}}{4}$.

For a quadrilateral, split along a diagonal whose length is known and
add the two triangle areas.

:::

A short verse:

> *Add the sides; halve them — call it $s$.*
> *Subtract each side from $s$; multiply all four by $s$.*
> *The square root of that product is the area.*
> *No height, no angle — only sides.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Find the area of a triangular field with sides $13$ m, $14$ m,
> $15$ m. (This is a famous Heronian triangle — try to spot why.)

A competent solver:

1. $s = \dfrac{13 + 14 + 15}{2} = 21$.
2. $s - 13 = 8, \quad s - 14 = 7, \quad s - 15 = 6$.
3. Area $= \sqrt{21 \cdot 8 \cdot 7 \cdot 6} = \sqrt{7056} = 84$ m$^2$.

**Answer:** $84$ m$^2$. (The triangle is *Heronian* — integer sides and
integer area. Discovered by Heron in his *Metrica*.)

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Find the area of a triangle with sides $6, 8, 10$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Notice $6^2 + 8^2 = 100 = 10^2$ — right triangle! Two ways to compute.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
By Heron: $s = 12$, area $= \sqrt{12 \cdot 6 \cdot 4 \cdot 2} =
\sqrt{576} = 24$. Or by base-height: $\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 6 \cdot 8 = 24$.
Both agree. **24 sq.units.**
</details>

**W2.** Find the area of an equilateral triangle with side $10$ cm.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{10^2 \sqrt{3}}{4} = 25 \sqrt{3} \approx 43.3$ sq.cm.
</details>

**W3.** A triangle has sides $9, 12, 15$. Compute the semi-perimeter and
state without computing whether the area is an integer.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$9^2 + 12^2 = 225 = 15^2$ — right triangle.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$s = 18$. Area = $\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 9 \cdot 12 = 54$ — integer.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A triangular plot has sides $50, 65, 65$ metres. Find its area.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Isosceles: $s = 90$. Then $s - 50 = 40, s - 65 = 25$ (twice).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Area $= \sqrt{90 \cdot 40 \cdot 25 \cdot 25} = 25 \cdot \sqrt{3600} =
25 \cdot 60 = 1500$ m$^2$.
</details>

**P2.** A quadrilateral garden has $AB = 3, BC = 4, CD = 4, DA = 5$ m
and the diagonal $AC = 5$ m. Find its area.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\triangle ABC$ has sides $3, 4, 5$. $\triangle ACD$ has sides $5, 4, 5$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\triangle ABC$: right triangle, area $= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 3 \cdot 4 = 6$.
$\triangle ACD$: $s = 7$, area $= \sqrt{7 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 2} =
\sqrt{84} = 2\sqrt{21} \approx 9.17$. Total $\approx 15.17$ m$^2$.
</details>

**P3.** The perimeter of a triangle is $30$ cm; its sides are in the
ratio $3 : 4 : 5$. Find the area.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let sides be $3k, 4k, 5k$. Sum = $12k = 30 \Rightarrow k = 2.5$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sides: $7.5, 10, 12.5$. Right triangle (the ratios are Pythagorean).
Area = $\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 7.5 \cdot 10 = 37.5$ sq.cm. (Check by
Heron: $s = 15$, $\sqrt{15 \cdot 7.5 \cdot 5 \cdot 2.5} =
\sqrt{1406.25} = 37.5$ ✓.)
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** A triangle has perimeter $48$ cm and sides in the ratio
$3 : 4 : 5$. A second triangle has perimeter $48$ cm and is equilateral.
Which has the larger area?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
First: same as P3 scaled. Second: equilateral with side $16$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
First: sides $12, 16, 20$. Right triangle. Area $= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 12
\cdot 16 = 96$ sq.cm.
Second: $\tfrac{16^2 \sqrt{3}}{4} = 64 \sqrt{3} \approx 110.85$ sq.cm.
**The equilateral wins.** (For fixed perimeter, equilateral maximises
the area — the *isoperimetric* bound for triangles.)
</details>

**S2.** A field is in the shape of a quadrilateral $ABCD$ with
$AB = 30, BC = 40, CD = 35, DA = 25$ m. The diagonal $AC = 50$ m. Find
the area.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Two triangles. $\triangle ABC$ has $30, 40, 50$ — Pythagorean.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\triangle ABC$: right triangle, area $= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 30 \cdot 40
= 600$ m$^2$.
$\triangle ACD$: sides $50, 35, 25$. $s = 55$. Area $=
\sqrt{55 \cdot 5 \cdot 20 \cdot 30} = \sqrt{165000} \approx 406.2$ m$^2$.
Total $\approx 1006.2$ m$^2$.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** The semi-perimeter of a triangle with sides $5,
6, 7$ is:
(a) $9$ (b) $18$ (c) $9.5$ (d) $7$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(5 + 6 + 7)/2 = 9$. **(a).**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** Find the area of a triangle whose sides are
$26, 28, 30$ cm.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$s = 42$. Area $= \sqrt{42 \cdot 16 \cdot 14 \cdot 12} =
\sqrt{112896} = 336$ sq.cm.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** The sides of a triangular plot are in
ratio $3 : 5 : 7$ and its perimeter is $300$ m. Find its area.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$3k + 5k + 7k = 300 \Rightarrow k = 20$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sides: $60, 100, 140$. $s = 150$. Area $= \sqrt{150 \cdot 90 \cdot 50
\cdot 10} = \sqrt{6750000} = 1500\sqrt{3}$ m$^2 \approx 2598.1$ m$^2$.
</details>

**4-mark (long answer).** A field is in the form of a triangle with
sides $50$ m, $80$ m, and $90$ m. Find the area of the field, and the
length of the perpendicular dropped from the vertex opposite to the
side of $90$ m.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use Heron for the area; then equate to $\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 90 \cdot h$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$s = 110$. Area $= \sqrt{110 \cdot 60 \cdot 30 \cdot 20} =
\sqrt{3960000} = 600\sqrt{11}$ m$^2 \approx 1989.97$ m$^2$.
For the height: $\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 90 \cdot h = 600\sqrt{11}
\Rightarrow h = \tfrac{40\sqrt{11}}{3} \approx 44.22$ m.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Class 9 Exemplar Ch. 10, Problem 10.3
(Heron's on a quadrilateral by triangulation, similar to P2).
**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE Class 9 sample paper "find area of a
plot with sides $a, b, c$" (similar to 4-mark above).
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Heron's formula needs an angle or a height."** No — only the
   three sides. That is the whole point.
2. **"Three lengths always make a triangle."** Only if each pair sums
   to more than the third. If $a + b \le c$, the formula gives zero or
   complex; you should reject as "not a triangle".
3. **"For a quadrilateral, you can plug all four sides into the
   formula."** No — Heron's formula is for triangles only. For a
   quadrilateral, split into two triangles along a diagonal and use the
   formula on each.
:::

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you state Heron's formula and apply it to fresh sides cold?
2. Did you solve W1–P3 with at most one hint each?
3. Could you split a quadrilateral into two triangles and find its area
   without prompting?

- **3 yes:** move on to **Surface Areas and Volumes**.
- **2 yes:** redo S1 and S2.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* 3 and 5.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`t1.area-perimeter-volume`](../../tier-1-foundations/13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd)
  — basic area meaning.
- [`cbse9.triangles`](../07-triangles/index.qmd) — three sides
  (uniquely) determine a triangle.
- [`cbse8.squares-and-square-roots`](../../tier-cbse-08-class-8/05-squares-and-square-roots/index.qmd)
  — simplifying $\sqrt{n}$.

### What this unlocks

- [`cbse9.surface-areas-and-volumes`](../11-surface-areas-and-volumes/index.qmd)
  — flat-face area sums for prisms.
- [`cbse10.areas-related-to-circles`](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/12-areas-related-to-circles/index.qmd)
  — sector & segment areas of circles, and combined figures.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 9, Chapter 10 — Heron's Formula** (NCERT 2023 rationalised
  syllabus). Covers Section 10.1: the formula and its applications.
- **Sister synthesis lesson** — the synthesis curriculum treats area at
  a more general level via
  [`t1.area-perimeter-volume`](../../tier-1-foundations/13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd).
  Heron's formula proper is unique to this NCERT chapter.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Triangle meshes are the universal data structure
for 3D graphics. Computing the area of each triangle (for lighting
weights, collision masses, level-of-detail decisions) is done by Heron's
formula or its 3D vector-cross-product equivalent — the latter is
literally Heron's formula in disguise.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research / surveying.** Heron's formula is how surveyors estimated
land area from rope-and-stake measurements for two thousand years before
GPS. Modern GIS still triangulates terrain ("triangulated irregular
network") and sums Heron areas to compute parcel sizes from satellite
imagery.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones honoured:** Pestalozzi (cut-out triangle Touch),
  Bhāskara (one-verse compactification), AoPS (Heronian triangles like
  $13, 14, 15$ as a recurring motif).
:::
